elements.sent-2411-001: With artificial intelligence (AI) being well established within the daily lives of research communities, we turn our gaze toward an application area that appears intuitively unsuited for probabilistic decision-making: the area of formal methods (FM).
elements.sent-2411-002: FM aim to provide sound and understandable reasoning about problems in computer science, which seemingly collides with the black-box nature that inhibits many AI approaches.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, many researchers have crossed this gap and applied AI techniques to enhance FM approaches.
elements.sent-2411-004: As this dichotomy of FM and AI sparked our interest, we conducted a systematic mapping study to map the current landscape of research publications.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this study, we investigate the previous five years of applied AI to FM (2019--2023), as these correspond to periods of high activity.
elements.sent-2411-006: This investigation results in 189 entries, which we explore in more detail to find current trends, highlight research gaps, and give suggestions for future research.
elements.sent-2411-007: Artificial intelligence (AI), and especially the subdomain of machine learning (ML), is becoming increasingly relevant for all industries and increasingly penetrates research communities, as several studies show~\cite{Lu2019,Elahi2023,Jiang2023,Chang2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Consequently, AI applications also gained popularity in software development ~\cite{barenkamp2020applications,shafiq2021literature .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In this study, we ask whether this trend is also observable within the formal methods communities.
elements.sent-2411-010: Formal methods (FM) correspond to a mathematically rigorous approach to software and systems development in which the concern is to provide a formal representation of a hardware, software, or systems engineering problem~\cite{Abran2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This representation then lends itself to mathematical assessments such as correctness, soundness, or well-definedness.
elements.sent-2411-012: Furthermore, these assessments can be easily reproduced and followed by the practitioner.
elements.sent-2411-013: The needed guarantees and the rigorousness of these methods seem to stand in tension with AI applications, which often show nondeterministic behavior and mostly correspond to black-box techniques.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, much like in software development, AI-driven tools might still supplement the formal development process without impeding the correctness of the results.
elements.sent-2411-015: And from personal experience, we noticed an increase in publications applying AI techniques to FM in recent years, suggesting that the FM field is following the overall trend as well.
elements.sent-2411-016: To substantiate this and to obtain an overview of which FM subdomains already utilize AI, we performed this systematic mapping study (SMS)~\cite{petersen2015guidelines .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The primary goal is to observe the development of AI applications quantitatively to the field of FM\@.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this work, we report on our journey and the results of our SMS, accessing the quantitative nature of the topic of AI application in the FM domain.
elements.sent-2411-019: This represents a typical goal for this kind of study, as pointed out by \citet{Kitchenham2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Mainly, we are interested in which FM domains are targeted by AI improvement already, which AI or ML applications have been investigated in the literature so far, where potential research gaps exist, and whether there appears to be consensus within the FM domains of which AI techniques seem most beneficial.
elements.sent-2411-021: A challenge to this assessment is the wide variety of topics on both sides.
elements.sent-2411-022: AI and FM are huge topics, and their potential overlap may be considerable.
elements.sent-2411-023: This study aims to be the first assessment of the general research landscape, i.e., we assume a mainly quantitative lens on the topic.
elements.sent-2411-024: Consequently, we highlight the areas which received more attention in the literature as well as those that received less attention.
elements.sent-2411-025: We refrain, however, from doing a qualitative assessment (e.g., a systematic literature review) our goal is to gain an initial overview of the field's landscape.
elements.sent-2411-026: While our search process resulted in a data set of 457 highly relevant publications covering more than 50 years of research applying AI to FM, we investigated only articles from the last five years in more detail.
elements.sent-2411-027: This period is chosen as it corresponds to a peak in the number of publications as indicated by previously mentioned studies and also allows us to focus on the most recent developments.
elements.sent-2411-028: Consequently, we end up with a tally of 191 primary studies, two of which are pure data set presentations.
elements.sent-2411-029: We analyzed the remaining 189 studies to determine which AI techniques were found to be applicable in which FM areas.
elements.sent-2411-030: Further, we derive research suggestions from our insights by pointing out research gaps that might further mature the field.
elements.sent-2411-031: Both the data set of the last five years as well as the complete data set of 457 publications are made publicly available to interested researchers at \url{https://github.com/hhu-stups/ai4fm-studies .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The rest of this mapping study is structured as follows: \Cref{sec:background introduces the relevant subdomains of FM and AI\@.}
elements.sent-2411-033: It will also provide an overview of our understanding of AI\@.
elements.sent-2411-034: We present our leading research questions in \cref{sec:goal and detail our search and selection process in \cref{sec:strategy .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: In \cref{sec:threats , we discuss potential threats to the validity of our conducted systematic mapping study.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Systematic mapping studies, as proposed by Kitchenham et al.~\cite{Kitchenham2007 , aim to assess the quantitative nature of a research field.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Here, the goal is to give an overview of the available research.
elements.sent-2411-039: SMS complement systematic literature reviews by comparing research contributions and their qualitative impact.
elements.sent-2411-040: SMS are typically performed using a multi-step approach.
elements.sent-2411-041: First, the field of interest is selected.
elements.sent-2411-042: Then, research questions are formulated, and inclusion criteria (IC) and exclusion criteria (EC) are defined.
elements.sent-2411-043: IC and EC will serve as filters to help select relevant results.
elements.sent-2411-044: After that, the search query is crafted.
elements.sent-2411-045: The goal is to apply the search query to the scientific meta-search engines to retrieve a good corpus of contributions that answer the research questions.
elements.sent-2411-046: With the retrieved corpus, the IC and EC are applied.
elements.sent-2411-047: If indicated, snowballing is done, usually until a stable closure of the corpus is reached.
elements.sent-2411-048: With a corpus constructed, the aim is to answer the research questions.
elements.sent-2411-049: Formal methods (FM) describe a mathematically rigorous approach to design software as well as hardware systems, and are concerned with analysis, validation, and verification at any part of the respective system's life cycle~\cite{woodcock2009formal,clarke1996formal .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Employing FM allows to formulate precise statements of desired functionality or requirements in form of a formal model (or formal specification) while not constraining the possible implementation thereof.
elements.sent-2411-051: The formal model is represented in a mathematically approachable form which lends itself to reasoning and hence allows rigorous analysis of critical properties such as correctness, safety, soundness, or well-definedness.
elements.sent-2411-052: In the following, we overview FM creation and reasoning methods most relevant to this works.
elements.sent-2411-053: Model checking~\cite{Baier2008, Clarke2018 explores a program's or systems' state space.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The state space sets all possible value constellations the program can achieve.
elements.sent-2411-055: In its most basic form, explicit state model checking, model checking aims to explore all reachable states and check if they are faulty, i.e., if they violate any specified properties.
elements.sent-2411-056: If this is the case, a counter-example is found.
elements.sent-2411-057: Otherwise, the system works correctly, as no faulty states are reachable.
elements.sent-2411-058: There are many flavors besides explicit state model checking.
elements.sent-2411-059: Noteworthy for this study are linear temporal logic (LTL) model checking, symbolic model checking, and statistical model checking.
elements.sent-2411-060: LTL model checking encodes temporal properties of interest into a formula that is checked against execution traces of the state space.
elements.sent-2411-061: Symbolic model checking abstracts the state space and makes a symbolic evaluation of a complex expression, resulting in the ability to handle even infinite state spaces for the price of precision.
elements.sent-2411-062: Statistical model checking investigates state spaces created by assigning probabilities on transitions between states and provide probabilistic guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-063: A core application of theorem proving (TP) is to evaluate statements about a formal model's internal consistency and behavioral integrity.
elements.sent-2411-064: When talking about theorem proving, we usually assume some formal representation of the problem for which proofs are formulated and discharged.
elements.sent-2411-065: Discharging proofs can either be done by a fully automatic theorem prover (ATP)~\cite{Gallier2015 that finds a solution on its own, or an interactive theorem prover (ITP)~\cite{Blazy2013 that requires human input for individual proof steps and transformations.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: ITPs provide a user interface to keep track of progress, sub-goals, and available properties.
elements.sent-2411-067: The Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT)~\cite{Biere2009 refers to finding the right model for a boolean formula to satisfy the formula.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Historically, SAT was the first problem found to be NP-complete and has since drawn a broad research interest.
elements.sent-2411-069: Satisfiability modulo theories (SMT)~\cite{Barrett2018 is a more generalized form of the SAT which enriches the problem setting with various theories such as arithmetics, data structures, or set theory.}
elements.sent-2411-070: In the context of FM, SAT and SMT solving find applications in theorem proving~\cite{Brown2013 , bounded model checking~\cite{Shtrichman2000 , equivalence checking~\cite{Goldberg2001 or test generation~\cite{Zeng2005 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Under the term of synthesis, we group all attempts automatically or semi-automatically create (parts of) formal models during the formal development process, such as the generation of models from natural language specification or vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-072: We further group the idea of automated model repair under this aspect, where for a model with some violation, a fix is synthesized that rectifies the violation.
elements.sent-2411-073: In the scope of this mapping study, we may encounter FM approaches and techniques, which do not belong to any previous group.
elements.sent-2411-074: We will group these as other FM techniques.
elements.sent-2411-075: For instance, this includes meta-approaches such as selecting the right verification algorithm, general program analysis, or termination analysis.
elements.sent-2411-076: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a catch-all phrase for various techniques and approaches that aim to imitate intelligent decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-077: They further emphasize the problem with the term itself, pointing out the difficulty of precisely defining what it means for a human to be intelligent.
elements.sent-2411-078: Nevertheless, AI is regularly used in daily life and the research community.
elements.sent-2411-079: An important AI paradigm is machine learning (ML).
elements.sent-2411-080: The goal of ML algorithms is to observe and extract patterns within the data which can then be applied to the automation of a given task ~\cite{mitchell1997machine .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Instead of manually defining a set of rules for the pattern recognition, machine learning algorithms uncover such rules in an automated fashion, a process referred to as learning.
elements.sent-2411-082: ML algorithms experience the given data in some manner and improve their exhibited performance to solve the task at hand gradually, the more experience they gather.
elements.sent-2411-083: Below, we list and briefly explain AI and ML algorithms which are most relevant to this mapping study.
elements.sent-2411-084: For the reader familiar with AI, the selection seems mostly ML rather than AI.
elements.sent-2411-085: Indeed, we aimed to find applications of AI to FM, and the search query we will discuss later was prepared accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, in order to bring a result up front, most contributions use some form of ML.
elements.sent-2411-087: Neural networks (NNs)~\cite{rosenblatt1962principles build the foundation of what is known today as the area of deep learning~\cite{goodfellow2016deep .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: A deep neural network (DNN) consists of a layer of inputs, a layer of outputs, and one or more hidden layers.
elements.sent-2411-089: Inputs are forwarded layer-wise and combined first by a weighted sum and a follow-up non-linear transformation.
elements.sent-2411-090: The clue is the training of a DNN, which starts the network with randomly initialized weights for the summations above but adjusts them gradually in the learning process to converge to the desired function.
elements.sent-2411-091: A considerable benefit of neural networks lies in their variety.

elements.sent-2411-093: The most important for this work are convolutional neural networks (CNNs)~\cite{lecun2010convolutional for images, recurrent neural networks (RNNs)~\cite{jordan1997serial for sequential data, the transformer architecture~\cite{Vaswani2017 for language processing, and graph neural networks (GNNs)~\cite{Zhou2020 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Today, deep learning is considered one of the most popular topics in ML~\cite{sarker2021deep .}
elements.sent-2411-095: Reinforcement learning (RL)~\cite{Kaelbling1996 is foremost a machine learning paradigm.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Given an environment, a set of actions that change the environment, and a reward function that quantifies a given environmental state, the RL agent's goal is to learn a policy over their available actions which maximizes the cumulative reward over time.

elements.sent-2411-098: Natural language processing (NLP)~\cite{Chowdhary2020 describes the research area of, as the name suggests, processing and evaluating texts of human language.}

elements.sent-2411-101: Genetic and evolutionary algorithms (EAs)~\cite{mitchell1998introduction describe a family of optimization algorithms that are rooted in the phenomenon of evolution.}
elements.sent-2411-102: An EA starts with a randomly assembled population of candidates for a given optimization problem and a fitness function.
elements.sent-2411-103: The fitness function measures how well a candidate solves the problem.

elements.sent-2411-110: The considered statistical approaches include support vector machines (SVM)~\cite{Kecman2005 , logistic and linear regression (LR)~\cite{montgomery2021introduction , k nearest neighbors (KNN)~\cite{cover1967nearest , support vector machines (SVM)~\cite{cortes1995support , decision trees (DT)~\cite{breiman1984classification and random forests (RF)~\cite{breiman2001random , or Bayesian inference approaches~\cite{Tipping2004 .}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-356: While datasets are publicly available for TP and SAT solving, the number of usable, established, and available benchmark data sets is minuscule for other areas.
elements.sent-2411-357: Generally, we see a current trend where researchers use datasets as they see fit or even generate data without referencing the procedure or providing the data set afterward.

elements.sent-2411-360: Concluding, we see a strong need for unified defaults to not only allow reproducibility but also to introduce familiar benchmarks into the field.

elements.sent-2411-363: From the given analysis, we can derive five areas of growth potential, which we outline below.
elements.paragraph-2411-005: Development of unified benchmarks and data sets
elements.sent-2411-364: As pointed out in our discussion to \cref{rq:datasets , we are concerned with the lack of established data sets and benchmarks in the FM su-communities.}
elements.sent-2411-365: While for TP, a good amount of contributions exist already tackling this problem, the other areas seemingly fall short.
elements.sent-2411-366: This creates room for reproducibility issues between research groups and hinders a more precise comparison between results as established benchmarks could provide.
elements.sent-2411-367: Developing strong baselines might not only increase comparability between different methods but might also reduce the entry hurdle for new researchers due to publicly available data sets.
elements.sent-2411-368: The main challenge is to create data sets which can be used for different formalisms.
elements.sent-2411-369: This would allow smaller communities to still benefit from available data which might be posed in a more popular formalism while furthering research on their area.
elements.sent-2411-370: An orientation on how such a unified benchmark environment could look like would be OpenAIGym~\cite{Brockman2016 , a Toolkit for RL to foster research by enhancing reproducibility.}
elements.paragraph-2411-006: Investigate the benefits of data mining for FM
elements.sent-2411-371: In the age of big data, we were expecting more studies that apply data mining techniques, be it for feature engineering or performance analytical approaches.
elements.sent-2411-373: While it might very well be that the kind of problems solvable by data mining are not relevant to the FM communities, it is also possible that potential benefits are not well-known.
elements.sent-2411-374: We propose to research if and how data mining approaches can benefit FM.
elements.sent-2411-375: First, this would highlight potential benefits to the community.
elements.sent-2411-376: Second, due to the investigative nature of data mining, this might strengthen our insides in our tools and best practices.
elements.sent-2411-377: Consequently, this might further the quality of other AI applications in FM due to new knowledge.
elements.paragraph-2411-007: Application of LLMs and generative AI
elements.sent-2411-378: While LLMs, at this point, are established tools that found their way into daily applications, we only found little use of them so far in our corpus of studies.
elements.sent-2411-379: This can be due to their relatively high employment costs.
elements.sent-2411-380: Another reason could be that respective studies are still in the process of being published and were hence invisible to our systematic mapping study.
elements.sent-2411-381: Nevertheless, we see strong potential for generative AI approaches, especially in the areas of synthesis and auto-formalization.
elements.sent-2411-382: However, also simple test-case generation or the generation of documentation for existing models seem promising starting points to build trust in the process.
elements.sent-2411-383: We envision substantial benefits from applying generative AI during FM development and point out the need for more research.
elements.sent-2411-384: One can easily envision tool chains of automatic generation, checking, and documentation of formal models with reproducible and provable results.
elements.sent-2411-385: However, we also see the need for more exploration regarding how generative AI can be helpful besides text production.
elements.sent-2411-386: For instance, how could generative AI be leveraged in proof mining? What stages in the formal development process can benefit from generated inputs, and what types could and should be generated? Pure generation of code and text might not be the ceiling for FM.
elements.paragraph-2411-008: Increase the potentials of AI in model checking
elements.sent-2411-387: Another underrepresented topic is model checking.
elements.sent-2411-388: Anecdotally, we know model checking had much EA applied in the past while it was a seemingly unpopular application area for AI in our five-year corpus.
elements.sent-2411-389: However, we see a significant potential to revisit respective research with a modern lens.
elements.sent-2411-390: Especially in the context of bounded model checking (BMC) or statistical model checking (SMC) and faulty state prediction.
elements.sent-2411-391: BMC cuts off parts of a model's state space for performance and computability.
elements.sent-2411-392: SMC checks states until a certain confidence in the absence of faulty states is reached.
elements.sent-2411-393: Both approaches already do not fully traverse the state space and can only indicate the absence of faults.
elements.sent-2411-394: AI-enforced model checking could alleviate BMC, SMC, and model checking as a whole by predicting potential faulty states or needed search depths, learning search policies via RL to reach faulty states in a more targeted manner, or predict equivalence classes of states for search space reduction.
elements.sent-2411-395: A study of this type and extent can only be conducted with the possibility of encountering threats that threaten validity.
elements.sent-2411-396: We orient ourselves at Zhou et al.~\citet{Zhou2020 for addressing the most critical ones.}

elements.sent-2411-450: Context: With artificial intelligence (AI) being well established within the daily lives of research communities, we turn our gaze toward formal methods (FM).
elements.sent-2411-451: FM aim to provide sound and verifiable reasoning about problems in computer science.
elements.sent-2411-452: Objective: We conduct a systematic mapping study to overview the current landscape of research publications that apply AI to FM\@.
elements.sent-2411-453: We aim to identify how FM can benefit from AI techniques and highlight areas for further research.
elements.sent-2411-454: Our focus lies on the previous five years (2019--2023) of research.
elements.sent-2411-455: Method: Following the proposed guidelines for systematic mapping studies, we searched for relevant publications in four major databases, defined inclusion and exclusion criteria, and applied extensive snowballing to uncover potential additional sources.
elements.sent-2411-456: Results: This investigation results in 189 entries which we explored to find current trends and highlight research gaps.
elements.sent-2411-457: We find a strong focus on AI in the area of theorem proving while other subfields of FM are less represented.
elements.sent-2411-458: Conclusions: The mapping study provides a quantitative overview of the modern state of AI application in FM\@.
elements.sent-2411-459: The current trend of the field is yet to mature.
elements.sent-2411-460: Many primary studies focus on practical application, yet we identify a lack of theoretical groundwork, standard benchmarks, or case studies.
elements.sent-2411-461: Further, we identify issues regarding shared training data sets and standard benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-462: This study asks whether this trend is also observable within the formal methods communities.
elements.sent-2411-463: Formal methods (FM) correspond to a mathematically rigorous approach to software and systems development in which the concern is to provide a formal representation of hardware, software, or systems engineering problem~\cite{Abran2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-464: These assessments can be easily reproduced and followed by the practitioner.
elements.sent-2411-465: However, the needed guarantees and the rigorousness of these methods seem to conflict with AI applications, which often show nondeterministic behavior and mostly correspond to black-box techniques.
elements.sent-2411-466: The trade-off appears obvious: Injecting AI's nondeterminism and unpredictability into FM's rigorousness could lead to faster results and proofs with less human intervention at the price of fundamental guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-467: This point of tension seems interesting and needs further investigation, but it is not the only way how AI can enrich FM\@.
elements.sent-2411-468: Much like in software development, AI-driven tools might still supplement the formal development process without impeding the correctness of the results.
elements.sent-2411-469: From personal experience, we noticed increased publications applying AI techniques to FM in recent years, suggesting that the FM field also follows the overall trend.
elements.sent-2411-470: In this work, we report on our journey and the results of our SMS, accessing the quantitative nature of the topic of AI applications in the FM domain.
elements.sent-2411-471: A challenge in this assessment is the wide variety of topics on both sides.
elements.sent-2411-472: Consequently, we highlight areas that received more attention in the literature, as well as those that received less attention.
elements.sent-2411-473: However, we refrain from doing a qualitative assessment, e.g., a systematic review of the literature.
elements.sent-2411-474: Our goal is to gain an initial overview of the field's landscape.
elements.sent-2411-475: Consequently, we have 191 primary studies, two of which are pure data set presentations.
elements.sent-2411-476: Both the data set of the last five years and the complete data set of 457 publications are made publicly available to interested researchers at \url{https://github.com/hhu-stups/ai4fm-studies .}
elements.sent-2411-477: Systematic mapping studies (SMS), as proposed by Kitchenham et al.~\cite{Kitchenham2007 , aim to assess the quantitative nature of a research field.}
elements.sent-2411-478: The counterpart to an SMS would be a systematic literature review where the main goal is to assess the quality of contributions.
elements.sent-2411-479: IC and EC serve as filters over the collected primary studies to help select relevant results.
elements.sent-2411-480: The goal is to apply the search query to the scientific meta-search engines to retrieve a good corpus of primary studies that answer the research questions.
elements.sent-2411-481: Formal methods (FM) describe a mathematically rigorous approach to design and assess software as well as hardware systems, and are concerned with analysis, validation, and verification at any part of the respective system's life cycle~\cite{woodcock2009formal,clarke1996formal .}

elements.sent-2411-489: Symbolic model checking abstracts the state space and makes a symbolic evaluation of a complex expression; thus, it shrinks the overall state space for the price of precision.
